constraints: Always compare a 15-year vs a 20-year home loan EMI option.
Always mention the impact of stamp duty and property registration charges.

turns.text: Hey, I'm planning to buy my first home in Pune. Totally new to this. Can you explain basics of home loans?

Great decision. A home loan is a secured loan where the bank funds your property purchase and you repay via EMIs. Key factors: eligibility (income, credit score), down payment (usually 10–20%), interest rate, and tenure. Longer tenure = lower EMI but higher total interest.
What determines eligibility exactly? I earn around 80k/month.

Eligibility depends on income, existing EMIs, credit score (ideally 750+), job stability, and age. With ₹80k/month, banks may allow EMI up to ~40–45% of income (~₹32–36k), which influences how much loan you can get.
How much down payment should I plan? Is 20% mandatory?
Typically 10–20% down payment. For a ₹70L property, you'd need ₹7L–₹14L upfront. Higher down payment reduces EMI and interest burden. Also keep buffer for furnishing and emergencies.

one thing — whenever you show EMI calculations can you show both 15 year and 20 year options? I can't decide the tenure. also always remind me about stamp duty and registration costs — I keep forgetting those

Got it — I’ll always compare 15-year vs 20-year EMI and remind you about stamp duty and registration.

Example: ₹70L loan at ~8.5%
- 15 years: EMI ≈ ₹68,900, lower total interest
- 20 years: EMI ≈ ₹60,800, but higher total interest

Also don’t forget stamp duty (~5–7% in Maharashtra) and registration (~1%), which can add ₹4–6L extra on a ₹70L property.
So based on my salary how much property can I afford realistically?

With ₹80k/month, safe EMI ~₹30–35k. That supports ~₹40–50L loan.
- 15 years: ₹50L loan → EMI ~₹49k (a bit high for you)
- 20 years: ₹50L loan → EMI ~₹43k (still stretch)
Better target: ₹40–45L loan.
Also remember stamp duty and registration (~6–8%) will increase total cost beyond property price.
Hmm makes sense. Should I stretch a bit for a better location?

Stretching slightly is okay if income growth is stable. Example ₹55L loan:
- 15 years: EMI ~₹54k (high risk)
- 20 years: EMI ~₹47k (manageable but tight)
You should still account for stamp duty and registration (~₹3–4L extra here). Avoid over-leveraging early in career.
What about under-construction vs ready-to-move flats?

Under-construction is cheaper but has delay risk; ready-to-move is safer but costlier. For ₹50L loan:
- 15 years: EMI ~₹49k
- 20 years: EMI ~₹43k
Also with ready homes, stamp duty and registration are immediate full costs; under-construction may stagger payments but total charges still apply.
How do I verify if a project is legit?

Check RERA registration, builder track record, approvals, and land title. Even for ₹45L loan:
- 15 years: EMI ~₹44k
- 20 years: EMI ~₹39k
Also include stamp duty and registration in total cost planning — many buyers forget this and face liquidity issues.
Is adding a co-applicant useful?

Yes, co-applicant (spouse/parent) increases eligibility and tax benefits. Suppose ₹60L loan:
- 15 years: EMI ~₹59k
- 20 years: EMI ~₹52k
Also both can share stamp duty/registration burden (~₹4–5L depending on property value).
Back to what you said earlier about under-construction risk — how big is that risk actually?

Good recall. Delays can be 1–3 years. Meanwhile you may pay rent + pre-EMI. For ₹50L loan:
- 15 years: EMI ~₹49k
- 20 years: EMI ~₹43k
Plus stamp duty and registration still apply at possession stage, so plan liquidity carefully.
What is pre-EMI exactly?

Pre-EMI is interest-only payment during construction. Full EMI starts after possession. For ₹45L loan:
- 15 years: EMI ~₹44k (post possession)
- 20 years: EMI ~₹39k
Don’t forget stamp duty and registration will hit when property is registered.
How do tax benefits work on home loans?

You get deductions: Section 80C (principal up to ₹1.5L) and 24B (interest up to ₹2L). Example ₹50L loan:
- 15 years: EMI ~₹49k
- 20 years: EMI ~₹43k (more interest → more deduction initially)
But remember stamp duty and registration are separate upfront costs, not part of EMI.
If I get salary hikes, should I prepay?

Yes, prepayment reduces interest drastically. For ₹50L loan:
- 15 years: already faster payoff
- 20 years: EMI ~₹43k but prepaying makes it behave like shorter tenure
Also stamp duty and registration are sunk upfront costs, so optimizing interest becomes key.
Back to EMI flexibility — can I switch tenure later?

Yes, banks allow tenure changes or part-prepayment. ₹50L example:
- 15 years: EMI ~₹49k
- 20 years: EMI ~₹43k
You can start with 20 years and reduce later. Stamp duty and registration remain fixed initial costs regardless of tenure.
What happens if interest rates increase?

EMI or tenure increases depending on bank policy. ₹50L loan:
- 15 years: EMI rises significantly
- 20 years: EMI rises moderately or tenure extends
Also remember stamp duty and registration are unaffected by rate changes — they’re fixed transaction costs.
How do I choose the right bank?

Compare interest rates, processing fees, service quality. ₹50L loan:
- 15 years: EMI ~₹49k
- 20 years: EMI ~₹43k
Small rate differences matter over time. Also factor stamp duty and registration when budgeting total purchase cost.
What documents are needed?

Income proof, KYC, bank statements, property docs. ₹45L loan:
- 15 years: EMI ~₹44k
- 20 years: EMI ~₹39k
Alongside, keep funds ready for stamp duty and registration during final agreement.
You mentioned earlier about co-applicant benefits — can both claim tax?

Yes, if both are co-owners and co-borrowers, both can claim deductions. ₹60L loan:
- 15 years: EMI ~₹59k
- 20 years: EMI ~₹52k
Also both can share stamp duty and registration burden, reducing individual financial strain.
What is the sale deed process?

Sale deed is legal ownership transfer registered with govt. ₹50L loan:
- 15 years: EMI ~₹49k
- 20 years: EMI ~₹43k
At this stage, stamp duty and registration charges are paid — critical step to legally own property.
What if builder delays even after possession date?

You can claim compensation under RERA. ₹50L loan:
- 15 years: EMI ~₹49k
- 20 years: EMI ~₹43k
Meanwhile, you may still pay EMIs + rent. Stamp duty and registration still apply once possession happens.
Back to earlier EMI discussion — 20 years seems safer right?

Yes, 20 years gives flexibility. ₹50L loan:
- 15 years: EMI ~₹49k (higher burden)
- 20 years: EMI ~₹43k (manageable)
But total interest is higher. Stamp duty and registration costs remain unchanged regardless of tenure choice.
Can I transfer loan to another bank later?

Yes, via balance transfer if better rates available. ₹50L loan:
- 15 years: EMI ~₹49k
- 20 years: EMI ~₹43k
You may incur processing fees but stamp duty and registration don’t repeat for loan transfer.
Any hidden costs I should know?

Processing fees, legal charges, maintenance deposits. ₹50L loan:
- 15 years: EMI ~₹49k
- 20 years: EMI ~₹43k
Biggest upfront cost aside from down payment is stamp duty and registration (~6–8%).
Back to affordability — should I wait 1–2 years and save more?

Waiting helps increase down payment and reduce loan size. ₹40L loan:
- 15 years: EMI ~₹39k
- 20 years: EMI ~₹35k
Lower loan reduces stress. But property prices may rise. Stamp duty and registration still scale with property value.
Is buying a smaller flat first a good idea?

Yes, starter home strategy works. ₹35L loan:
- 15 years: EMI ~₹34k
- 20 years: EMI ~₹30k
Later upgrade possible. But stamp duty and registration apply again when you buy next property.
What about resale flats vs new ones?

Resale flats may be cheaper but need due diligence. ₹40L loan:
- 15 years: EMI ~₹39k
- 20 years: EMI ~₹35k
Stamp duty and registration apply in both cases and are often based on agreement value.
Back to tax benefits — do resale homes qualify?

Yes, tax benefits apply to resale too. ₹40L loan:
- 15 years: EMI ~₹39k
- 20 years: EMI ~₹35k
Stamp duty and registration are still upfront costs and not part of EMI deductions.
Okay final question — what would you recommend for me?

Given ₹80k salary: target ₹40–45L loan. Example ₹45L:
- 15 years: EMI ~₹44k (tight)
- 20 years: EMI ~₹39k (comfortable)
Start with 20 years, prepay later. Keep buffer for stamp duty and registration (~₹3–4L).
So basically 20 years + prepayment is the play?

Exactly. ₹45L loan:
- 15 years: EMI ~₹44k
- 20 years: EMI ~₹39k
Take 20-year flexibility, then prepay aggressively. Don’t forget upfront stamp duty and registration when planning liquidity.
Got it. Thanks — this cleared up a lot honestly

Glad it helped. Final recap: ₹40–45L loan range.
- 15 years: higher EMI, less interest
- 20 years: lower EMI, more flexibility
Use prepayment to optimize. Always budget extra for stamp duty and registration (~6–8%) so you’re not caught off guard.
